topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt: One of the numbers $1$, $2$, $3$ is written in each cell of a rectangular table with $4$ rows and $n$ columns. For every three different columns there is a row that intersects them at cells with different numbers. Find the maximum $n$ for which there exists such a table.
response_text:
Let the 4 rows be \(R_1,R_2,R_3,R_4\).  
Each column is a 4-tuple from \(\{1,2,3\}^4\).

The condition says:

> For any 3 distinct columns, there exists a row in which the entries in those 3 columns are all different.

So for any triple of columns, in at least one row the three symbols are exactly \(\{1,2,3\}\).

We seek the largest possible number \(n\) of columns.

## Reformulation
For each row \(i\), define a function \(f_i\) on the set of columns, taking values in \(\{1,2,3\}\): \(f_i(c)\) is the entry in row \(i\), column \(c\).

Then the condition becomes:

For any 3 distinct columns \(a,b,c\), there exists \(i\in\{1,2,3,4\}\) such that
\[
f_i(a),f_i(b),f_i(c)
\]
are pairwise distinct.

Equivalently, if we identify each column with a word of length 4 over \(\{1,2,3\}\), then among any 3 chosen words, in some coordinate the three symbols are \(1,2,3\).

This is exactly a 3-perfect hash family of length 4 over an alphabet of size 3. We now prove the maximum size is \(9\).

---

## Upper bound: \(n\le 9\)

Consider only rows \(R_1,R_2\).  
These two rows classify columns into \(3^2=9\) types according to the ordered pair
\[
(R_1,R_2)\in\{1,2,3\}^2.
\]

Suppose there were \(10\) columns. By pigeonhole, two columns \(x,y\) have the same pair in rows 1 and 2.

Now look at rows 3 and 4. The pair \((R_3,R_4)\) of a column also has only 9 possible values.

If there were a third column \(z\) with \((R_3,R_4)(z)=(R_3,R_4)(x)\), then for the triple \(x,y,z\):

- in rows 1 and 2, columns \(x,y\) agree, so those three entries cannot all be different;
- in rows 3 and 4, columns \(x,z\) agree, so again those three entries cannot all be different.

Hence such a triple would violate the condition.

Therefore, once a pair \(x,y\) coincides in rows 1 and 2, all columns must have distinct pairs in rows 3 and 4. In particular there can be at most \(9\) columns total.

So \(n\le 9\).

---

## Construction for \(n=9\)

We will exhibit 9 columns. Index the columns by pairs \((a,b)\in\{1,2,3\}^2\), and define the 4 entries in that column by
\[
(a,\ b,\ a+b,\ a+2b)\pmod 3,
\]
where we interpret residues \(0,1,2\) as symbols \(3,1,2\) respectively (equivalently work over \(\mathbb F_3\)).

In table form, the 9 columns are all vectors
\[
(a,b,a+b,a+2b),\qquad a,b\in \mathbb F_3.
\]

Why does this work? Take any 3 distinct columns corresponding to \((a_1,b_1),(a_2,b_2),(a_3,b_3)\in\mathbb F_3^2\).

We must show that for some one of the 4 rows, the three values are all distinct.  
Assume the contrary: in each of the 4 rows, the 3 values are not all distinct, so in each row at least two of the three columns agree.

That means for each of the four linear forms
\[
a,\quad b,\quad a+b,\quad a+2b,
\]
two of the three points \((a_j,b_j)\) give the same value.

But in \(\mathbb F_3^2\), the level sets of these four forms are exactly the four families of parallel lines of slopes \(\infty,0,-1,-2\).  
Any two distinct points determine one line in exactly one of these directions, so among 3 distinct points, at most 3 of these 4 directions can witness an equality pair. Thus it is impossible that all 4 rows fail simultaneously.

Hence for some row, the 3 entries are pairwise distinct, as required.

So \(n=9\) is achievable.

Therefore the maximum is

Final answer: \(9\)
